Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The paper explicitly gives MtBAN as GenBank accession AY184243 and AtBAN as AF092912.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The title and Results explicitly conclude that BAN encodes anthocyanidin reductase (ANR).

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper states BAN has amino acid sequence similarity to dihydroflavonol reductase (DFR) and shows activity with NADPH/NADH, supporting a DFR-like NAD(P)H-dependent reductase in the flavonoid pathway.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: BAN is directly placed in flavonoid/condensed tannin (proanthocyanidin) biosynthesis throughout the paper.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper describes BAN as functioning in the CT branch downstream of anthocyanidin formation.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Results state an alternative model places BAN immediately downstream of ANS, and discuss BAN competing with anthocyanin formation for pathway flux.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: BAN is shown to form flavan-3-ols such as epicatechin that are described as components/units of condensed tannins.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Recombinant BAN was incubated with cyanidin, pelargonidin, and delphinidin, which are explicitly identified as substrates.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper identifies products as epicatechin from cyanidin, (-)-epiafzelechin from pelargonidin, and putative epigallocatechin from delphinidin; these are described as 2,3-cis-flavan-3-ols.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The central biochemical finding is that BAN reduces anthocyanidins to corresponding 2,3-cis-flavan-3-ols using NADPH or NADH.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports BAN as a branch-switch enzyme affecting flux between anthocyanins and CTs, but does not directly establish it as a key control step beyond this functional role, and explicitly does not test rate limitation.

Source_Species.reason: The studied BAN genes are from Arabidopsis and Medicago truncatula.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper explicitly names Arabidopsis thaliana and Medicago truncatula.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Arabidopsis Col-0 appears in figures for transgenic/mutant material, but the paper does not clearly state Col-0 as the source background for AtBAN itself; no variety is given for M. truncatula.

Applied_Species.reason: BAN was expressed in tobacco and Arabidopsis transgenics and recombinant protein was expressed in Escherichia coli.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper explicitly names Nicotiana tabacum, Arabidopsis thaliana, and Escherichia coli.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 is explicitly shown for Arabidopsis controls, but no variety/strain details are given for tobacco or E. coli in the main text.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The paper states constitutive expression under control of the cauliflower mosaic virus 35S promoter.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: MtBAN expression in young seeds, flowers, buds, and leaves is explicitly described; AtBAN expression in seed endothelium from fertilization to preglobular embryo stage is also stated.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results state several transgenic lines exhibited high ectopic BAN transcript expression in leaves of tobacco and Arabidopsis.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The paper directly connects BAN to condensed tannins/proanthocyanidins and to flavan-3-ol units including (-)-epicatechin.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Condensed tannins/proanthocyanidins are explicitly discussed as flavonoids.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: CTs are described in Arabidopsis seed coat/endothelium context, induced in transgenic tobacco petals, and induced in transgenic Arabidopsis leaves.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The introduction explicitly states beneficial health effects, antioxidant activity, contribution to flavor/astringency via epicatechin, protection against pasture bloat, and improved nitrogen nutrition.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Ectopic BAN expression reduced anthocyanin pigmentation and led to CT accumulation in tobacco petals and Arabidopsis leaves.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed approaches are reported: recombinant E. coli assays, HPLC/HPLC-MS/GC-MS/NMR/CD for product identification, transgenic overexpression, DMACA staining, butanol-HCl CT quantification, and mutant comparisons/inference with ban and tt18/ANS.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: ANS is discussed as an upstream pathway enzyme whose position/function relates to BAN and as competing for pathway flux, but no direct protein-protein interaction is shown.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The paper directly reports loss of pink flower pigmentation/anthocyanins in tobacco petals upon BAN expression, indicating flux diversion from anthocyanins.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The Discussion explicitly proposes engineering BAN for bloat-safe alfalfa, improved protein utilization, reduced greenhouse gas and urine-N losses, and modification of health properties, flavor, and astringency.

Omics_Data.reason: The paper states MtBAN was identified by random sequencing of a M. truncatula seed cDNA library and provides no global omics accession beyond the gene accession.

Other_Important_Info.reason: All components are stated in the paper: prior LAR hypothesis lacked evidence and is overturned, AtBAN and MtBAN gave identical products, minor trans products are attributed to epimerization artifacts, and BAN is single copy in M. truncatula.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: This is an accurate synthesis of the main Results: BAN is ANR rather than LAR, converts anthocyanidins to corresponding cis-flavan-3-ols including epicatechin, ectopic expression lowers anthocyanins and induces CTs, and BAN functions immediately downstream of ANS toward PA/CT biosynthesis.
